summary: Rods are responsible for dim light (scotopic) vision and peripheral vision, while cones mediate colour and fine detail (photopic) vision.
Rods mediate colour vision and cones are responsible for dim light vision.
Both rods and cones function equally in dim and bright light.
Sound is transmitted through the ossicular chain (malleus, incus, stapes) which provides impedance matching between air and cochlear fluid.
Sound is transmitted directly from the tympanic membrane to the cochlear fluid without ossicles.
The ossicular chain reduces sound pressure to protect the inner ear.
The organ of Corti in the cochlea contains hair cells; high frequencies are detected at the base and low frequencies at the apex.
High-frequency sounds are detected at the apex and low-frequency sounds at the base of the cochlea.
The organ of Corti is located in the semicircular canals.
Taste from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is carried by CN VII (facial), and posterior one-third by CN IX (glossopharyngeal).
Taste from the anterior two-thirds is carried by CN IX and posterior one-third by CN VII.
All taste sensation is carried by the vagus nerve (CN X).
Semicircular canals detect angular (rotational) acceleration, while the utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration.
Semicircular canals detect linear acceleration and otolith organs detect angular acceleration.
All vestibular organs detect only angular acceleration.
The fovea centralis contains the highest concentration of cones and is the area of maximum visual acuity.
The fovea contains mostly rods and has the lowest visual acuity.
The optic disc is the area of maximum visual acuity.
There are three types of cones sensitive to red, green, and blue wavelengths, and colour blindness results from absence or abnormality of one type.
There are five types of cones, each sensitive to a different colour.
Colour vision depends on rods, not cones.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Revise the sensory modalities.
